coredata.dc:title: Discrimination of tetracycline in water using a fluorescence probe based on UiO-67 Encapsulated with NCDs

Zhang, Yanqiu
Peng, Mingguo
coredata.dc:description: The large-scale production and abuse of antibiotics cause environmental pollution, threaten human health, and destroy the ecological balance. Efficient detection of these pollutants has emerged as a prominent topic in current research. In this study, we developed a novel material called UiO-67-NCDs by combining UiO-67 with nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) for sensing trace amounts of tetracycline (TC) in water. The NCDs were successfully introduced onto UiO-67 by the post-synthesis modification, resulting in a strong blue fluorescence at 445 nm. Thanks to the hydrogen bonding, π-π interactions and the internal filter effect (IFE) between UiO-67 and NCDs, UiO-67-NCDs was capable for TC sensing, exhibiting fluorescence quenching in 10 s with a limit of detection (LOD) of 32.9 nM (0–38 μM). The visual detection capabilities, high sensitivity, rapid detection time, and simplicity of the fluorescent probe make it a promising candidate for antibiotic detection in aqueous environments. This will further stimulate the interest of researchers in the in-depth study of MOFs and quantum dots and their applications in sensing.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Tetracycline
Fluorescent sensing
Nitrogen-doped carbon
UiO-67

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence sensing of TC by UiO-67-NCDs
The detection of TC in real water samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Photos (a) UiO-67 and (b) UiO-67-NCDs under natural light; SEM image of (c) UiO-67, and (d)-(e) UiO-67-NCDs. (f)-(h) TEM images of UiO-67-NCDs with different amplify folds.
(a) XRD patterns; (b) FTIR spectra and (c) XPS spectrum of UiO-67 and UiO-67-NCDs.
(a) Emission spectra of UiO-67, NCDs, and UiO-67-NCDs under 357 nm excitation; (b) The chromaticity diagrams of NCDs and UiO-67-NCDs under 357 nm excitation.
(a) Fluorescence spectra of UiO-67-NCDs mixed with different concentrations of TC (Ex = 357 nm); (b) Calibration curve of fluorescence intensity (I) and TC concentration.

The relative fluorescence intensity of UiO-67-NCDs and (a) metal ions (108 μM) and (b) anions (108 μM) coexisting in aqueous solution at 445 nm; the relative fluorescence intensity of UiO-67-NCDs and TC (108 μM) coexisted with (a) metal ions (108 μM) and (b) anions (108 μM) in aqueous solution at 445 nm.

(a) XRD patterns of UiO-67-NCDs and UiO-67-NCDs after detection TC; (b) XPS patterns of UiO-67-NCDs and UiO-67-NCDs after detection TC; The C1s spectrum of (c) UiO-67-NCDs and (d) UiO-67-NCDs after detection TC; The O1s spectrum of (e) UiO-67-NCDs and (f) UiO-67-NCDs after detection TC.

(a) Fluorescence decay curves of UiO-67-NCDs (red ball) and UiO-67-NCDs in detecting TC (blue ball); (b) The excitation spectrum (red line, Em = 445 nm) and emission spectrum (green line, Ex = 357 nm) of UiO-67-NCDs, and the UV–visible absorption spectrum (purple line) of TC. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Comparison of UiO-67-NCDs with other fluorescence probes for the detection of TC.
Standard recovery for detecting TC in water samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Discrimination of tetracycline in water using a fluorescence probe based on UiO-67 Encapsulated with NCDs

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environmental Science and Engineering, Changzhou University, Changzhou 213164, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Environmental Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Environmental Science and Engineering, Changzhou University, Changzhou 213164, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Urban Construction, Changzhou University, Changzhou 213164, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Urban Construction, Changzhou University, Changzhou 213164, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The large-scale production and abuse of antibiotics cause environmental pollution, threaten human health, and destroy the ecological balance. Efficient detection of these pollutants has emerged as a prominent topic in current research. In this study, we developed a novel material called UiO-67-NCDs by combining UiO-67 with nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) for sensing trace amounts of tetracycline (TC) in water. The NCDs were successfully introduced onto UiO-67 by the post-synthesis modification, resulting in a strong blue fluorescence at 445 nm. Thanks to the hydrogen bonding, π-π interactions and the internal filter effect (IFE) between UiO-67 and NCDs, UiO-67-NCDs was capable for TC sensing, exhibiting fluorescence quenching in 10 s with a limit of detection (LOD) of 32.9 nM (0–38 μM). The visual detection capabilities, high sensitivity, rapid detection time, and simplicity of the fluorescent probe make it a promising candidate for antibiotic detection in aqueous environments. This will further stimulate the interest of researchers in the in-depth study of MOFs and quantum dots and their applications in sensing.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With the development of industry, a large amount chemicals used in industrial production are discharged into aquatic ecosystems, posing a great threat to aquatic ecosystems and environmental health [1]. Antibiotics have become an indispensable therapeutic tool in human society for combating pathogens. However, studies have demonstrated that approximately 30 % −90 % of antibiotics cannot be fully utilized by the human and are excreted through feces and urine [2]. Consequently, these residual antibiotics gradually penetrate the environment, subsequently accumulating in humans and animals with the ecosystem’s circulation, thereby damaging their immune systems and threatening their lives [3]. Tetracyclines (TC), as a class of antibiotics extensively employed in the treatment of zoonoses, may pose potential risks to humans and ecosystems because of their wide distribution in the environment and long-lasting biological activity [4,5]. In the context of increasingly prominent global environmental pollution, the detection of residual antibiotics in the environment has emerged as a crucial research direction [6–10]. Therefore, the exploitation of a speediness, precise, sensitive, and highly selective tetracycline detection method is crucial in ensuring public health and environmental safety.

Traditional methods for antibiotic detection, for example liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry [11,12], high-performance liquid chromatography [13–15], and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay [16,17], exhibit high sensitivity and accuracy. However, their widespread application in rapid detection and field settings is limited due to the requirement of complex sample pretreatment, expensive equipment, and skilled operators. In contrast, fluorescence sensors on the basis of metal–organic frameworks (MOFs) exhibit the merits of facile operation, rapid response, and high sensitivity, thereby demonstrating significant potential in the realm of environmental monitoring [18–22]. Nevertheless, achieving superior fluorescence sensing relying on the self-luminescence of MOFs is challenging. To address this issue, the incorporation of quantum dots [23,24], lanthanide metal ions [25–27], or fluorescent dyes [28,29] into MOFs has been employed to construct highly performing fluorescent sensors. As a typical Zr-based MOF, UiO-67 stands out as an ideal choice for constructing fluorescence sensors due to its simple synthesis method, simple raw materials availability, excellent chemical stability, and adjustable pore environment [30–32]. Nitrogen-doped carbon dots, a type of carbon nanomaterials with a size smaller than 10 nm, enjoy significant application potential in biological imaging, photocatalysis and sensing thanks to their excellent optical properties, good biocompatibility, environmental friendliness, and easy functionalization [33–35]. Particularly, in sensing applications, NCDs find wide usage in detecting various pollutants because of their unique fluorescence properties and high sensitivity [36–38].

This study designed a composite material called UiO-67-NCDs, which can effectively detect TC in water by utilizing the synergistic advantages of MOFs and NCDs. UiO-67-NCDs emitted stable bright blue fluorescence emission under excitation at 357 nm. In the presence of TC, the emission of UiO-67-NCDs declined due to the static quenching upon the hydrogen bonding and π-π interactions. The fluorescence response kinetics and the limit of detection were analyzed through a series of experiments. X-ray diffraction (XRD), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), ultraviolet–visible spectroscopy (UV–vis), and fluorescence spectra analyses were used to investigate the recognition mechanism of UiO-67-NCDs towards TC molecules. In addition, potential application prospects were evaluated through interference experiments, real water sample tests, and circulation experiments.
The materials and instrumental methods used in the study are listed in the Supplementary Information.

According to the previous method, some modifications were made to synthesize NCDs [38]. Citric acid (5.45 mol) and ethylenediamine (1 mL) were added in 10 mL deionized water and stirred vigorously for 30 min, followed by transfer into a 25 mL polytetrafluoroethylene-lined autoclave and heated at 200 ℃ for 5 h. Following cooling to ordinary temperature, a brown-black solution was obtained and subjected to centrifugation to eliminate any particulate matter.

UiO-67 was prepared via a solvothermal method following the established procedure [31]. The mixture of 1 mmol of ZrCl4, 1 mmol of 4,4′-biphenyl dicarboxylic acid, 10 mmol of acetic acid, 30 mL of DMF, and 12 mol of HCl was prepared in a 100 mL flask and dissolved completely with sonication. Subsequently, they were equally distributed into four 25 mL polytetrafluoroethylene-lined autoclaves and heated 24 h under120 ℃. After cooling down to ordinary temperature, the filtered sediments were washed three times with DMF and methanol. Then the samples were dried in a vacuum oven at 60° C for 6 h.

The UiO-67 (100 mg) and NCDs with varying volumes of 0.5, 1, 2, 3 mL were individually added to 30 mL of deionized water and stirred for 24 h, and respectively named as UiO-67–0.5NCDs, UiO-67-NCDs, UiO-67-2NCDs, and UiO-67-3NCDs. Subsequently, the mixture was centrifugated at 5000 r/min for 10 min, and then washed with deionized water until the filtrate was free of blue light under an ultraviolet lamp. Finally, materials were obtained after drying overnight in an oven at 60 ℃.

The response time and fluorescence stability of UiO-67-NCDs were evaluated as essential prerequisites for fluorescence detection of TC. Initially, dispersing UiO-67-NCDs (2 mg) in deionized0 water (2.5 mL) to assess the luminescence stability by recording the emission intensity of UiO-67-NCDs at different times within a day. Subsequently, dissolving UiO-67-NCDs (2 mg) in a series of deionized water (2.5 mL) with different pH (4–10) to investigate its pH stability through measurement of fluorescence emission intensity. Finally, the response kinetics were examined by dissolving 2 mg UiO-67-NCDs in a TC aqueous solution (2.5 mL, 108 μM) and monitoring the changes in fluorescence intensity. The mixed solution was stirred at a constant rate (180 r/min) during experiment to maintain its homogeneity. The pH of the 2.5 mL water containing 2 mg UiO-67-NCDs was determined to be 7.8, thus all subsequent experiments were conducted under pH = 7.8 conditions.

The TC fluorescence detection experiment was conducted by adding 2 mg UiO-67-NCDs to 2.5 mL of TC solution with varying concentrations, followed by recording the emission intensity after a specific response time. The tested UiO-67-NCDs were collected, soaked in deionized water, washed, and then dried before repeating the above experiments for five cycles to investigate their recyclability. Additionally, the selectiveness and anti-interference capability of UiO-67-NCDs to TC were studied. 2 mg UiO-67-NCDs was added into 2.5 mL solution containing different antibiotics (108 μM, GEM, SDZ, SMZ, ODZ, MDZ, AMC, THI, CPL), anions (108 μM, F−, Cl−, NO2 −, S2O3 2−, SO3 −, HSO4 −, HSO3 −, HCO3 −, CO3 2−, NO3 −, SO4 2−, PO4 3−, S2O8 2−) and metal ions (108 μM, K+, Na+, Al3+, Mg2+,Ca2+, Cu2+, Ba2+, Ni2+, Cd2+, Fe2+). After the response time, the fluorescence emission intensities were measured.

The practical application effectiveness of UiO-67-NCDs was assessed using lake water and reclaimed water samples. The lake water was sampled from a campus lake at Changzhou University, while the reclaimed water was obtained from the outlet of Changzhou Sewage Treatment Plant and filtered by a 0.45 μm microfiltration membrane. The collected water samples were used to prepare different concentrations of TC solutions, and the aforementioned experiments were repeated to measure the emission intensity. The accuracy of TC sensing by UiO-67-NCDs in environmental water samples was tested by the standard addition method.

The fluorescence performance of materials with varying mixing ratios was evaluated. As presented in Figure S1, the fluorescence emission peak intensity initially increased and then decreased with an increase in NCDs content, in which the UiO-67-NCDs with 1 mL NCDs added exhibited the highest fluorescence emission peak intensity, possibly due to aggregation of NCDs at higher concentrations leading to reduced fluorescence intensity. Therefore, UiO-67-NCDs was used for subsequent experiments.

The UiO-67 and UiO-67-NCDs synthesized in this study were characterized by a variety of instrument analyses. As depicted in Fig. 1 (a), under natural light, UiO-67 presented a white powder form, and the color changed to yellow after the introduction of NCDs (Fig. 1(b)). The surface morphologies were examined by SEM. According to Fig. 1(c), UiO-67 possessed an octahedral structure morphology. The particle size of UiO-67-NCDs was smaller compared to that of UiO-67 (Fig. 1(d)), which possibly attributed to the addition of NCDs limiting the spatial growth of UiO-67. Moreover, the microstructure of UiO-67-NCDs became a nanoflower-like shape covered with nanosheets (Fig. 1(e)). This phenomenon may be caused by the combination of NCDs and Zr4+ ions, which formed an NCD-Zr4+- NCD structure and hindered the formation of regular octahedral shape in UiO-67[39] and further combined with the –COOH on 4,4′-biphenyl dicarboxylic acid [40]. Additionally, TEM analysis was employed to elucidate the morphologies of UiO-67-NCDs. The distribution of UiO-67-NCDs diameters obtained was analyzed using Nano Measure software, revealing a predominant range of 240–300 nm (Fig. 1(f)). As shown in Fig. 1(g), UiO-67-NCDs exhibited a hierarchical structure coating with two-dimensional sheets, confirming the successful combination of NCDs with UiO-67. Furthermore, Fig. 1(h) illustrated that the fringe spacing value for NCDs in UiO-67-NCDs was measured as 0.303 nm, and the presence of bright lattice fringes indicated excellent crystallinity in the prepared NCDs. The changes in both macroscopic and microscopic morphology indicate successful binding between NCDs and UiO-67.

The crystal structures of UiO-67 and UiO-67-NCDs were examined using an X-ray diffractometer. According to Fig. 2 (a), the crystal diffraction peaks of UiO-67-NCDs showed similar diffraction peaks to that of UiO-67, indicating the preservation of the parent UiO-67 framework after NCDs loading. Moreover, different from UiO-67, the diffraction peak intensity of UiO-67-NCDs was slightly reduced, which can be attributed to the fact that NCDs occupied the internal void of UiO-67, resulting in a lower X-ray contrast between the porous skeleton and the cage.[41] The FTIR spectra of UiO-67 and UiO-67-NCDs were collected (Fig. 2(b)). The peaks observed at 668 and 769 cm−1 were ascribed to the stretching vibration and in-plane bending vibration of Zr-O, respectively. The strong peaks at 1420 and 1600 cm−1 were the same or reverse stretching of νas (–COO-) and νs (–COO-) groups [42]. This observation suggests the presence of UiO-67 in UiO-67-NCDs. Additionally, the spectrum of UiO-67-NCDs exhibited new peaks. For example, a novel peak that emerged at 1637 cm−1 was related to the vibration stretching of C = O bonds. Furthermore, a new peak emerged at 1466 cm−1 can be attributed to the bending vibration of N-H bonds within the urethane group present in the UiO-67-NCDs which further proved the combination of NCDs with the –COOH on 4,4′-biphenyl dicarboxylic acid. In addition, peaks corresponding to Zr3d, C1s, O2p from UiO-67, and N1s from NCDs appeared in the XPS spectrum of UiO-67-NCDs (Fig. 2(c)), indicating that UiO-67-NCDs retained the structural integrity of UiO-67 and combined with NCDs.

Under the excitation of 357 nm ultraviolet light, NCDs showed a blue fluorescence emission peak at 442 nm (Fig. 3 (a), blue line) and located in the blue district at a point (X = 0.15, Y = 0.12) on the CIE chromatic chart (Fig. 3 (b)). While UiO-67 displayed a fluorescence emission peak at 435 nm (Fig. 3 (a)) under the excitation of 357 nm. Upon loading NCDs on UiO-67, UiO-67-NCDs emitted strong blue fluorescence at 445 nm under the excitation of 357 nm ultraviolet light, which was situated at point b in the blue district of the CIE chromatic chart (X = 0.16, Y = 0.18). The quantum yield of UiO-67-NCDs was computed to be 45 % using rhodamine 6G as a standard.

The emission intensity of UiO-67-NCDs dispersed in water was measured at 445 nm, and no significant change was observed within 24 h (Figure S2). Furthermore, the emission intensity of UiO-67-NCDs at 445 nm remained stable in a pH range from 4 to 10 (Figure S3). These characteristics demonstrate the excellent optical stability and promising practical application potential of UiO-67-NCDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence sensing of TC by UiO-67-NCDs
ce:section.ce:para: The mixture of UiO-67-NCDs and TC was excited by a 357 nm ultraviolet lamp, and the emission intensity at 445 nm at different periods was recorded to explore its corresponding kinetics. As depicted in Figure S4, the fluorescence quenching of UiO-67-NCDs by TC was completed within 10 s and subsequently remained stable, which fully reflected the promising application potential of UiO-67-NCDs in TC sensing.

By gradually dropping the TC solution into UiO-67-NCDs, the fluorescence changes of UiO-67-NCDs were systematically investigated at varying concentrations of TC, thereby demonstrating the detection performance of this fluorescent probe. Under the excitation of 357 nm UV light, the emission intensity at 445 nm exhibited a negative correlation with increasing TC concentration (0–108 μM), achieving a remarkable quenching efficiency of 91.6 % (Fig. 4 (a)). At this time, the color of the mixture under the ultraviolet lamp also changed from bright blue to colorless (Fig. 4 (a) illustration, left picture before detection, right picture after detection). Moreover, a strong linear correlation (R 2 = 0.975) was observed between the fluorescence emission intensity and TC concentration from 0 to 37 μM, as determined by fitting with the Stern-Volmer equation (Eq. (1). The value of K SV was determined as 3.825 × 104 M−1 (Fig. 4 (b)) and the LOD of TC was calculated as 37.9 nM using the 3σ IUPAC criteria (3σ/slope), where σ represents the standard deviation of three blank measurements. It is noteworthy that the LOD achieved in this study for TC sensing surpasses that of most other TC detection platforms (Table 1 ).
(1)
I 0
/ I =
K
sv
M
+ 1
Where I 0 represents the emission intensities of UiO-67, I represents the emission intensities of UiO-67 after sensing TC; K sv represents the S-V constant (M−1); [M] represents the molar concentration of TC (μM).

The excellent sensor should possess superior selectiveness and anti-interference capabilities. Therefore, to better evaluate the selectiveness and anti-interference capabilities of UiO-67-NCDs, a diverse water matrix was simulated by introducing various ions and antibiotics into the solution. As depicted in Figure S5, the fluorescence of UiO-67-NCDs was quenched by TC, while other antibiotics showed the emission enhancement effect. This observation demonstrates the remarkable selectivity of UiO-67-NCDs. Moreover, in the presence of multiple interfering ions in water (Fig. 5 (a) and (b)), the fluorescence intensity of UiO-67-NCDs remained relatively stable. Furthermore, under coexistence with various ions in water along with TC, UiO-67-NCDs could accurately detect the presence of TC despite interference from these ions (Fig. 5(c) and (d)), indicating excellent anti-interference properties possessed by UiO-67-NCDs. Moreover, the TC detection experiments utilizing NCDs alone were performed and a reduction in fluorescence intensity upon the addition of TC was observed. As shown in Figure S6, when 108 μM of TC was introduced in 2.5 mL solution of containing 10 μL NCDs, the quenching efficiency reached 76 %, which is lower than that of UiO-67-NCDs. Additionally, interference experiments revealed that other antibiotics such as MDZ, CPL, ODZ, and SMZ also exhibited substantial quenching effects on NCD fluorescence (Figure S7). Consequently, the combination of UiO-67 and NCDs not only enhanced fluorescence quenching efficiency but also improved selectivity for TC detection.

The recycling performance of UiO-67-NCDs was investigated through five experimental cycles. Subsequently, Following the completion of these experiments, it was observed that UiO-67-NCDs still exhibited remarkable sensitivity towards TC with high quenching efficiency (Figure S8), indicating excellent recyclability potential for this sensor.

The recognition mechanism of TC molecules by UiO-67-NCDs was investigated through XRD, XPS, UV–vis absorption, and fluorescence spectra analyses. The crystal structure of UiO-67-NCDs remained unaltered after sensing TC, as depicted in the XRD patterns (Fig. 6 (a)). Moreover, the XPS spectrum of UiO-67-NCDs after the detection of TC exhibited characteristic peaks corresponding to C1s, O1s, N1s, and Zr3d (Fig. 6 (b)), demonstrating the preservation of the structure of UiO-67. The above results suggested that the fluorescence quenching observed in UiO-67-NCDs was not attributed to any structural damage. In addition, before and after TC detection, the C1s peak of UiO-67-NCDs exhibits peaks corresponding to C-C (284.8 eV), C = O (288.4 eV), C-N (287.1 eV), and π-π (290.6 eV) (Fig. 6(c) and (d)). However, the content of C = O decreased from 12.96 % to 11.16 % while the content of π-π increased from 1.65 % to 2.92 %. These findings suggest that during the process of TC detection, UiO-67-NCDs may enrich the trace amounts of TC through C = O and π-π interactions. The O1s peak exhibited changes before and after the detection of TC by UiO-67-NCDs. As illustrated in Figure (e)-(f), an additional O-H peak emerged on the spectrum after detection, and the Zr-O peak content decreased from 15.86 % to 7.86 %. These observations suggest that hydrogen bonding may also contribute to the enrichment of trace TC during the detection process.

The fluorescence lifetime test was employed to analyze whether the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) presented in the TC detection process. As depicted in Fig. 7 (a), the average fluorescence lifetime of UiO-67-NCDs solution was measured as 15.45 ns before the detection of TC, while after detection it decreased slightly to 14.55 ns. This marginal difference suggests that the quenching process primarily involves static quenching rather than FRET occurrence. To further investigate the mechanism of fluorescence response, UV–vis absorption spectroscopy was conducted. As illustrated in Fig. 7 (b), TC molecules exhibited a characteristic absorption peak within the range of 300–400 nm in their ultraviolet–visible absorption spectrum, which coincided with the excitation spectrum of UiO-67-NCDs at 350 nm, indicating an involvement of IFE in the fluorescence quenching process. These findings collectively demonstrated that during TC detection, UiO-67-NCDs enriched TC through hydrogen bonding and π-π interactions and quenched its fluorescence signal via IFE.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The detection of TC in real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: The accuracy of the UiO-67-NCDs sensor was tested by the standard addition method. The results presented in Table 2 demonstrated that both lake water and reclaimed water exhibit a recovery rate approaching 100 %, with a relative deviation of less than 5 %. These findings indicated that the sensor has the feasibility of application in the real environment.

In this work, a novel fluorescent material, UiO-67-NCDs, was successfully prepared and employed for sensing trace TC in water with high sensitivity and excellent selectivity. UiO-67-NCDs was synthesized by loading NCDs onto UiO-67 through post-modification and emitted bright blue fluorescence under excitation at 357 nm. Moreover, UiO-67-NCDs has essentially the same fluorescence intensity at different immersion times and different pH, indicating high fluorescence stability. During the detection process of TC, UiO-67-NCDs enriched low concentrations of TC in water through hydrogen bonding and π-π interactions, followed by fluorescence quenching via IFE to achieve TC detection with a limit of 37.9 nM, lower than many sensors currently used for TC sensing. Notably, UiO-67-NCDs displayed high selectivity towards TC detection with the appearance of common cations and anions in water. The accuracy of TC detection using UiO-67-NCDs was validated through standard addition experiments with environmental water samples, achieving a recovery rate of 99.9–107.7 %, which fully demonstrates the great potential of UiO-67-NCDs in practical applications. This study achieved efficient detection of TC through the design and optimization of composite materials, providing an effective technical means for trace antibiotic detection in the environment.

Yang Lu: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Software, Methodology, Investigation. Dechang Zeng: Validation, Data curation. Jiahui Zhao: Formal analysis, Data curation. Zihan Chang: Software. Xin Deng: Formal analysis. Xingyang Zhou: Software. Yangyang Hu: Software. Erdeng Du: Project administration. Yanqiu Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Mingguo Peng: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20210856) and National Students' Platform for Innovation and Entrepreneurship Training Program (202410292031Z).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2024.111962.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Antibiotics and antibiotic resistance genes in global lakes: A review and meta-analysis
